topics.name: Vocabulary Reasoning
topics.description: Use context, tone, and clue words to infer precise word meaning.

study.simpleMeaning: Vocabulary reasoning means finding a word's meaning from its sentence, tone, and surrounding clues.
study.whyItMatters: Aptitude tests often use familiar-looking words in precise contexts. Strong learners do not guess from memory alone; they prove meaning from the sentence.
strategy: Locate the target word and decide whether the sentence gives a definition, contrast, example, or cause.
Replace the word with a plain meaning and test whether the sentence still makes sense.
Check tone: positive, negative, neutral, formal, or cautious.
Reject choices that are related to the topic but do not fit the exact context.

solvedExample.prompt: The manager gave a candid report, admitting both the success and the delays.
solvedExample.explanation: The clue is admitting both success and delays. That points to openness, not politeness or speed.

commonTraps: Choosing a word that matches the subject but not the sentence logic.
Ignoring contrast words such as however, although, yet, and despite.
Choosing the strongest word when the sentence needs a moderate one.
study.tip: Before reading the options, say the meaning in your own simple words.

topics.name: Synonyms
topics.description: Choose the nearest meaning, not merely a related word.

study.simpleMeaning: A synonym question asks for the closest meaning of a word in standard use.
study.whyItMatters: High-quality synonym practice builds precision. Many wrong answers are connected to the word, but only one matches meaning, tone, and degree.
strategy: State the word's basic meaning.
Check whether the word is positive, negative, or neutral.
Compare degree: mild, strong, temporary, permanent, formal, or emotional.
Choose the nearest meaning, not the most dramatic answer.

solvedExample.prompt: Select the nearest synonym of concise.
solvedExample.explanation: Concise means giving much meaning in few words. Brief is nearest; vague and simple do not capture precision.

commonTraps: Choosing a word from the same topic area but different meaning.
Missing connotation, such as praise versus criticism.
Confusing strong and mild meanings.
study.tip: Ask: could this option replace the word in a careful sentence without changing meaning?

topics.name: Antonyms
topics.description: Identify the most accurate opposite by meaning and degree.

study.simpleMeaning: An antonym is the word that most nearly means the opposite of the target word.
study.whyItMatters: Antonym questions test whether you understand the direction and strength of meaning. The best opposite is precise, not merely different.
strategy: Define the target word in plain language.
Name the opposite idea before checking choices.
Check degree: the opposite of mild is not always extreme.
Avoid unrelated words that only feel negative or positive.

solvedExample.explanation: Reluctant means unwilling or hesitant. Eager gives the opposite attitude.

commonTraps: Choosing a word with the same negative tone instead of the opposite meaning.
Choosing an unrelated word because it sounds unfamiliar.
Missing the degree of the word.
study.tip: Convert the target word into a short phrase, then reverse that phrase.

topics.name: Analogies
topics.description: Match relationships between word pairs with careful logic.

study.simpleMeaning: An analogy asks you to identify the relationship between two words and apply the same relationship to another pair.
study.whyItMatters: Analogies build flexible reasoning. They reward learners who name the relationship, not learners who only notice topic similarity.
strategy: Write a precise bridge sentence for the first pair.
Check the direction of the relationship.
Match the relationship type, not just the subject area.
Reject pairs with reversed, weaker, or merely associated relationships.

solvedExample.explanation: A thermometer measures temperature; a barometer measures pressure. The relationship is instrument to what it measures.

commonTraps: Matching topic instead of relationship.
Reversing the order of the pair.
Choosing a related object rather than the function.
study.tip: Use a bridge sentence: X is to Y as A is to B.

topics.name: Word Relationships
topics.description: Recognize category, function, part-whole, cause-effect, and degree relationships.

study.simpleMeaning: Word relationships describe how two words are logically connected.
study.whyItMatters: This topic supports analogies, reading comprehension, and sentence logic because precise relationships reduce guessing.
strategy: Ask whether one word is a type, part, tool, result, quality, location, or opposite of the other.
Turn the relationship into a short sentence.
Check whether the relationship works in both directions or only one direction.
Separate real meaning from loose association.

solvedExample.prompt: What is the relationship between catalyst and reaction?
solvedExample.answer: A catalyst starts or speeds a reaction.
solvedExample.explanation: The relationship is cause or agent to process. A catalyst is not a part of the reaction and not merely a synonym.

commonTraps: Calling every related pair a synonym.
Confusing category relationships with function relationships.
Ignoring direction, such as tool-to-task versus task-to-tool.
study.tip: If you can write a clear bridge sentence, the relationship is usually under control.

nodes.label: Bridge Sentence
nodes.text: X does Y, X is part of Y, or X causes Y

topics.name: Sentence Completion
topics.description: Use grammar, clue words, and logic to choose the best missing word or phrase.

study.simpleMeaning: Sentence completion means filling a blank with the word or phrase that best fits the sentence's logic.
study.whyItMatters: The blank is rarely solved by vocabulary alone. The sentence usually gives signals such as contrast, cause, concession, or continuation.
strategy: Read the full sentence before looking at options.
Mark clue words such as although, because, therefore, yet, and similarly.
Predict the blank in simple words.
Choose the option that fits both meaning and grammar.

solvedExample.prompt: Although the proposal sounded ambitious, the committee found it ____ because each step had a clear budget and timeline.
solvedExample.explanation: Although signals contrast. Ambitious could sound unrealistic, but clear budget and timeline make it practical.

commonTraps: Ignoring contrast signals.
Choosing a word that fits one phrase but not the whole sentence.
Missing whether the blank needs a positive or negative word.
study.tip: Cover the options and predict the blank first.

topics.name: Sentence Correction
topics.description: Improve grammar, clarity, concision, and sentence structure.

study.simpleMeaning: Sentence correction asks you to choose the clearest and most grammatically accurate version of a sentence.
study.whyItMatters: Good correction practice improves precision in writing and helps learners notice errors that affect meaning.
strategy: Identify the core subject and verb.
Check agreement, tense, pronoun reference, modifiers, comparisons, and parallel structure.
Prefer clear and concise wording over wordy phrasing.
Do not change the intended meaning unless the original is illogical.

solvedExample.prompt: Incorrect: Running late, the report was submitted by Amina.
solvedExample.answer: Running late, Amina submitted the report.
solvedExample.explanation: The original modifier makes it sound as if the report was running late. The corrected sentence attaches the modifier to Amina.

commonTraps: Choosing a grammatically correct option that changes meaning.
Missing misplaced modifiers.
Keeping wordiness because it sounds formal.
study.tip: Find the sentence's actor first; many correction errors hide in who is doing the action.

topics.name: Sentence Meaning and Logic
topics.description: Track how ideas connect inside a sentence or short statement.

study.simpleMeaning: Sentence meaning and logic is the skill of understanding how ideas in a sentence connect.
study.whyItMatters: Many verbal errors come from reading words correctly but missing the relationship between ideas. This topic builds control over implication, contrast, cause, and qualification.
strategy: Identify the main claim of the sentence.
Mark relationship signals such as because, however, therefore, unless, and whereas.
Separate what is stated from what is implied.
Choose the option that preserves the exact logic without adding a new claim.

solvedExample.prompt: Although the data are limited, they are consistent across three independent surveys.
solvedExample.answer: The sentence cautiously supports the data while admitting a limitation.
solvedExample.explanation: Although introduces a limitation; consistent across surveys provides support. The logic is balanced, not dismissive.

commonTraps: Treating cautious support as complete rejection.
Ignoring qualifying words such as some, most, often, may, and likely.
Adding information that the sentence does not state.
study.tip: Translate the sentence into a one-line logic map before choosing.

topics.name: Argument Analysis
topics.description: Separate conclusion, evidence, context, and assumptions before choosing an answer.

topics.name: Assumption Reasoning
topics.description: Find the unstated idea that connects evidence to a conclusion.

subTopics.name: What is an Assumption?
subTopics.description: Understand the hidden idea an argument depends on.

study.simpleMeaning: An assumption is an unstated idea that the argument treats as true.
study.whyItMatters: Most argument questions test whether you can notice what the writer did not say directly.
strategy: Find the conclusion.
Find the evidence.
Ask what must be accepted for the evidence to support the conclusion.
Avoid answers that are merely interesting.

solvedExample.argument: The school installed brighter corridor lights, so late arrivals will decrease.
solvedExample.answer: Late arrivals are partly caused by difficulty moving safely through dim corridors.
solvedExample.explanation: Without that link, brighter lights may not affect late arrivals.

commonTraps: Choosing a statement that supports the conclusion but is not required.
Choosing background information that does not connect evidence to conclusion.
study.tip: If the argument collapses when the answer is false, the answer is probably an assumption.

visual.assumption: Dim corridors contribute to late arrivals

subTopics.name: Necessary Assumption
subTopics.description: Identify what must be true for the argument to work.

study.simpleMeaning: A necessary assumption is an unstated idea that must be true for the argument to work.
study.whyItMatters: Necessary assumption questions reward precision. The correct answer is not just helpful; the conclusion depends on it.
strategy: Find the conclusion.
Find the evidence.
Ask: what must be true for this evidence to support this conclusion?
Remove options that are relevant but not required.
Use the negation test: if the opposite destroys the argument, the original choice is necessary.

solvedExample.argument: The city library added online renewals. Therefore, front-desk lines will become shorter.
solvedExample.answer: A meaningful number of front-desk visits are made by patrons renewing books.
solvedExample.explanation: If renewals are not a reason people stand in line, online renewals would not necessarily shorten the line.

commonTraps: Choosing an answer that would strengthen the argument but is stronger than required.
Choosing an answer that repeats the conclusion in different words.
Choosing a true-sounding detail that does not connect evidence to conclusion.
study.tip: Do not choose what merely supports the argument. Choose what the argument depends on.

subTopics.name: Missing Link
subTopics.description: Bridge the gap between evidence and conclusion.

study.simpleMeaning: A missing link is the bridge the writer forgot to state.
study.whyItMatters: When evidence and conclusion mention different ideas, the correct assumption often connects those ideas.
strategy: Underline the new idea in the conclusion.
Underline the evidence idea that is supposed to support it.
Ask what connects those two ideas.
Choose the smallest bridge that is required.

solvedExample.argument: The app sends reminders before study sessions. Therefore, students will complete more sessions.
solvedExample.answer: Forgetting sessions is one reason students fail to complete them.
solvedExample.explanation: The reminder only helps if forgetfulness is part of the problem.

commonTraps: Choosing an answer about motivation when the evidence is about memory.
Choosing a broad claim that goes beyond the argument.
study.tip: Name the gap in plain language before reading the answer choices.

subTopics.name: Trap Options
subTopics.description: Spot answers that sound helpful but are not necessary.

study.simpleMeaning: Trap options are attractive choices that do not actually hold the argument together.
study.whyItMatters: A calm learner can avoid choosing a fact that is useful, extreme, or interesting but not required.
strategy: Check whether the option is required, not merely helpful.
Beware of extreme words such as all, always, never, and only.
Reject choices that introduce a new goal.
Use the negation test when two choices feel close.

solvedExample.argument: A company switched to shorter training videos, so employees will finish training faster.
solvedExample.answer: Employees spend less total time watching the shorter videos than they spent with the older videos.
solvedExample.explanation: A trap might say employees enjoy short videos, but enjoyment is not required for faster completion.

commonTraps: Mistaking a benefit for a requirement.
Choosing a choice that is too broad for the conclusion.
study.tip: Ask, does the conclusion need this exact idea, or would it merely be nice to know?

visual.assumption: Shorter videos reduce total training time

subTopics.name: Assumption vs Inference
subTopics.description: Tell the difference between what must be true before the conclusion and what follows after the facts.

study.simpleMeaning: An assumption supports the argument from underneath. An inference is a conclusion you can draw from stated facts.
study.whyItMatters: Many wrong answers are valid inferences, but the question asks for the hidden support.
strategy: For assumptions, ask what the author needs.
For inferences, ask what the facts prove.
Check the question stem before choosing.
Do not swap a result for a requirement.

solvedExample.argument: The clinic added evening hours, so missed appointments will decline.
solvedExample.answer: Some missed appointments happen because patients cannot attend during daytime hours.
solvedExample.explanation: This is an assumption because the conclusion needs that hidden cause.

commonTraps: Choosing what probably follows from the evidence instead of what the author must assume.
Treating a prediction as a required bridge.
study.tip: Assumption: needed before the conclusion works. Inference: proven after the facts are accepted.

topics.name: Inference Reasoning
topics.description: Choose what follows from the facts without adding outside assumptions.

topics.name: Strengthen / Weaken Arguments
topics.description: Find evidence that makes an argument stronger or weaker.

topics.name: Logical Fallacies
topics.description: Recognize flawed reasoning patterns and tempting invalid conclusions.

topics.name: Cause and Effect
topics.description: Evaluate causal claims, alternate causes, and correlation traps.
